entries.shortExplanation: You are not descending low enough in the squat.
entries.fullExplanation: A full-depth squat brings your hip crease below the top of your knee. Stopping short reduces the amount of work your glutes and hamstrings do and masks real strength with momentum.
entries.whyItMatters: Shallow squats under-train the glutes and can create imbalances between quad-dominant and hip-dominant strength.
fixTips: Warm up your hip mobility — box-assisted squats or ankle rockers for 90s before your working sets.
Use a bench or box at your target depth as a tactile cue until depth becomes automatic.
Drop the weight 10–15% for two sessions and rebuild depth before chasing numbers again.

entries.shortExplanation: Your knees track inward toward each other during the descent or ascent.
entries.fullExplanation: Knee valgus is when the knees collapse toward the midline under load. It usually comes from weak external rotators at the hip, not weak knees themselves.
entries.whyItMatters: Repeated valgus loading stresses the medial knee ligaments and ACL, especially under heavy weight.
fixTips: Add banded clamshells and side-plank hip abduction to your warmup.
Cue: 'spread the floor apart' with your feet on every rep.
Briefly stop pushing the weights heavier until the valgus cleans up.

entries.shortExplanation: Your torso pitches forward more than the hip/knee angles require.
entries.fullExplanation: Some forward lean is normal in a low-bar squat, but excessive lean usually points to weak core bracing or tight ankles that force you onto your toes.
entries.whyItMatters: A torso dumped forward shifts load to the lumbar spine and makes it hard to hit depth without rounding.
fixTips: Test ankle dorsiflexion — if it's limited, use heeled lifting shoes or a plate under the heels.
Practice pause squats at mid-depth to reinforce an upright torso.
Brace harder before you unrack — breath in, tighten, then descend.

entries.shortExplanation: Your hips drift to one side out of the hole.
entries.fullExplanation: Hip shift means one side contributes more than the other coming up from the bottom. It's a strength or mobility asymmetry showing up under load.
entries.whyItMatters: Chronic hip shifts load one knee and SI joint harder than the other — a durability risk over many sets.
fixTips: Test single-leg strength (B-stance or split squats) to find the weaker side.
Add one extra set to the weaker side for 3–4 weeks.
Film from the front and replay to catch the shift you can't feel.

entries.shortExplanation: You stop short of fully extending the hips at the top.
entries.fullExplanation: Incomplete lockout means you re-descend before the glutes finish extending the hips. It shaves range of motion off every rep.
entries.whyItMatters: Missing the lockout under-develops the glutes and teaches bad patterns you'll carry into heavier sets.
fixTips: Add a half-second pause standing tall on each rep.
Cue: 'squeeze the glutes' at the top.
Use lighter back-off sets to drill full extension without the stimulus of a heavy load.

entries.shortExplanation: Rep cadence is faster than your targeted tempo.
entries.fullExplanation: Bouncing out of the hole or racing down can reduce time-under-tension and make reps less stable.
entries.whyItMatters: Faster reps leak tension and can hide control problems that surface when you add weight.
fixTips: Count 3 seconds down, 1 second pause, explosive up.
Use lighter sets to practice slow controlled eccentrics.
Film yourself and compare tempos.

entries.shortExplanation: Your hips drop toward the floor during the rep.
entries.fullExplanation: A sagging hip line means your core is not maintaining a straight plank position. The shoulders do more work while the trunk leaks force.
entries.whyItMatters: Sagging hips shift load into the lumbar spine and make the rep feel harder without actually training the triceps/chest more.
fixTips: Squeeze glutes and brace abs before every rep.
Regress to elevated pushups (hands on a bench) until you hold a straight line.
Add 2 sets of deadbugs to your warmup.

entries.shortExplanation: Your chest doesn't get close enough to the floor.
entries.fullExplanation: Depth in the pushup is measured by chest-to-floor distance. Most people cheat depth as fatigue sets in — the rep looks fine, but the range shrinks.
entries.whyItMatters: Partial reps leave work on the table. Chest and triceps under-train relative to the rep count you logged.
fixTips: Place a foam block under your chest as a depth marker.
Pause 1 second at the bottom on every rep.
If you fail a set, stop and note which rep lost depth — don't just grind more partials.

entries.shortExplanation: You don't fully extend the elbows at the top of the rep.
entries.fullExplanation: Bending the arms between reps keeps the triceps loaded but also skips the top half of the lift.
entries.whyItMatters: Skipping full extension misses end-range strength and makes the rep count misleading.
fixTips: Push to full elbow lockout on every rep.
Briefly pause at the top before the next rep.
If you can't lock out, reduce reps — form trumps volume.

entries.shortExplanation: One arm extends before the other.
entries.fullExplanation: Asymmetric pressing means one side finishes the rep while the other is still mid-push. It's usually a side-dominance pattern — your stronger arm takes over.
entries.whyItMatters: Chronic asymmetry creates imbalanced shoulder development and masks weakness on one side.
fixTips: Switch to alternating one-arm or elevated pushups to force the weak side to work.
Pause at lockout and feel both arms finish together.
Film from the head-on angle to see the asymmetry you can't feel.

entries.shortExplanation: Your elbows flare out to roughly 90° from the torso.
entries.fullExplanation: A wide elbow angle (close to 90°) puts the shoulder in a vulnerable impingement zone. A 45° angle is more shoulder-friendly.
entries.whyItMatters: Repeated flared elbows aggravate the rotator cuff and can lead to AC joint pain over time.
fixTips: Cue: 'tuck elbows toward ribs' on the descent.
Move hands slightly narrower than shoulder-width.
Strengthen scapular control with band pull-aparts between sets.

entries.shortExplanation: Rep tempo is faster than your target.
entries.fullExplanation: Racing through pushups trades control for bigger-looking set numbers.
entries.whyItMatters: Fast eccentrics skip productive time under tension and normalize momentum-driven reps.
fixTips: Count 3 seconds down, 1 second hold, 1 second up.
Lower rep targets in exchange for tempo adherence.
Pair fast and slow sets to feel the difference.

entries.shortExplanation: Chin never clears the bar at the top.
entries.fullExplanation: A complete pullup brings the chin over the bar at the top of every rep. Stopping short gives you a half-rep that undercounts the effort — and under-trains the upper back.
entries.whyItMatters: Partial pullups leave stretch and peak contraction strength undeveloped, and they count as full reps in your log.
fixTips: Use a resistance band for assistance until chin-over is automatic.
Drop reps — 3 perfect reps beat 8 partial reps.
Add scap pullups to your warmup to groove the pattern.

entries.shortExplanation: You don't return to a full dead hang between reps.
entries.fullExplanation: A full dead hang between reps resets the lats and preserves range. Short extensions let you use momentum and under-develop the bottom half of the strength curve.
entries.whyItMatters: Skipping the dead hang hides weakness in the stretched position and teaches a rep pattern that won't survive fatigue.
fixTips: Pause 1 second at the bottom, arms fully extended, before the next pull.
Drop rep count if needed — quality over quantity.
Do dead-hang holds on off days to build comfort in the bottom position.

entries.shortExplanation: Shoulders pull up toward the ears instead of back and down.
entries.fullExplanation: Shrugging during a pullup means your traps are taking over from your lats. A dialed pullup starts with the scapula depressing, then the arms pulling.
entries.whyItMatters: Shrugging under-trains the lats and can irritate the neck and upper traps under volume.
fixTips: Initiate each rep with scap pullups — pull shoulder blades down first, then bend elbows.
Think 'pull chest to bar' rather than 'pull chin up'.
Add band lat pulldowns for volume that reinforces the right pattern.

entries.shortExplanation: One side clears the bar before the other.
entries.fullExplanation: Asymmetric pulling is a side-dominance pattern — your stronger side takes more of the work.
entries.whyItMatters: Long-term asymmetry creates side-to-side strength gaps and skews posture.
fixTips: Add archer pullups or single-arm assisted pullups for the weak side.
Pause briefly at the top to feel both sides finish together.
Test with single-arm rows — the side that can do fewer reps is the weak side.

entries.shortExplanation: You drop too quickly from the top.
entries.fullExplanation: Dropping through the descent phase skips eccentric work — the portion where your lats are stretched under load and getting the strongest stimulus.
entries.whyItMatters: Eccentric work drives muscle growth. Skipping it on every rep leaves strength and hypertrophy gains on the table.
fixTips: Count 3 seconds down on every rep.
Drop reps if you can't control the descent.
Negatives-only sessions: jump to the top, descend slowly, repeat for 5 reps.

entries.shortExplanation: Your upper or lower back rounds under the load.
entries.fullExplanation: Any significant lumbar rounding under a deadlift is a red flag. The spine's neutral curve is what protects the discs when the glutes and hamstrings pull.
entries.whyItMatters: Repeated rounded-back deadlifting is a disc-injury pathway — even at moderate weights, it normalizes a pattern that will fail at heavier weights.
fixTips: Deload 30% and rebuild the hinge pattern before chasing numbers.
Add heavy rows and back extensions to strengthen the upper back.
Brace hard before every rep — breath in, rib cage down, tight lats.

entries.shortExplanation: Hips shoot up before the bar leaves the floor.
entries.fullExplanation: When the hips rise first, the lift becomes a stiff-leg deadlift by default — your glutes and hamstrings have to do the whole pull while your back is already horizontal.
entries.whyItMatters: It's a leading indicator for lumbar rounding and suggests the weight is too heavy for your current pulling strength.
fixTips: Drop the weight 15–20% and practice pushing the floor away with your legs on the initial pull.
Add pause deadlifts (1s pause just off the floor).
Strengthen quads with front squats — they help the initial pull.

entries.shortExplanation: Hips don't finish extending at the top.
entries.fullExplanation: A complete deadlift lockout has you standing tall with hips fully extended and glutes squeezed. Short lockouts leave work undone.
entries.whyItMatters: Short lockouts under-train the glutes at their top range and normalize partial reps that won't survive a competition or a 1RM test.
fixTips: Pause 1 second standing tall on every rep.
Add glute bridges and hip thrusts for top-range strength.
Cue: 'squeeze glutes until it hurts' at lockout.

entries.shortExplanation: One side of the bar comes up faster than the other.
entries.fullExplanation: Asymmetric pulls mean one side (usually your dominant) is taking more of the load, often paired with a hip shift.
entries.whyItMatters: Uneven pulling torques the spine asymmetrically — durable trouble over many sets.
fixTips: Add single-leg RDLs to find and strengthen the weaker side.
Film from directly in front — the shift is invisible from the side.
Reduce weight temporarily until both sides move together.

entries.shortExplanation: Spine rounds during the hinge.
entries.fullExplanation: In an RDL, neutral spine is non-negotiable. If the lower back rounds, you've exceeded your hamstring flexibility for the current weight.
entries.whyItMatters: Rounding under the load turns the RDL from a hamstring exercise into a lumbar-loading exercise — a disc-injury pathway.
fixTips: Stop the hinge where your spine would start to round, not where you run out of flexibility with a bend.
Add active hamstring mobility (Jefferson curls with no load, then light load).
Cue: 'push hips back' not 'bend forward'.

entries.shortExplanation: Knees bend too much, turning the RDL into a partial deadlift.
entries.fullExplanation: An RDL is a hip-hinge-dominant lift — knees should stay only slightly bent. Over-bending shifts the work off your hamstrings onto your quads.
entries.whyItMatters: Too much knee bend defeats the purpose of the lift. You're deadlifting, not RDL'ing.
fixTips: Start each rep with knees soft but locked in a fixed angle.
Cue: 'push hips back against a wall'.
Film from the side to see if knees track past their starting position.

entries.shortExplanation: You don't hinge far enough to stretch the hamstrings.
entries.fullExplanation: The point of the RDL is the hamstring stretch at the bottom. A shallow hinge skips the most productive part of the lift.
entries.whyItMatters: Short hinges under-train the hamstrings at length — the position where they grow.
fixTips: Use a plate to tap the floor at each rep's bottom.
Check hamstring mobility — if it's tight, add 2 sets of dynamic hip flows to the warmup.
Lower the weight until you hit full depth consistently.

entries.shortExplanation: One hip hinges deeper or faster than the other.
entries.fullExplanation: Asymmetric hinges reveal hamstring length differences between sides. It's common but worth fixing.
entries.whyItMatters: Long-term side dominance creates compensatory patterns up the chain (hip, SI, low back).
fixTips: Swap in single-leg RDLs for 3–4 sessions.
Work hamstring mobility on the tighter side first in warmup.
Brace core harder — a soft trunk lets the asymmetry surface.

entries.shortExplanation: The hinge is too fast to control the stretch.
entries.fullExplanation: RDLs benefit hugely from slow eccentrics. Rushing kills the hamstring stretch reflex benefit.
entries.whyItMatters: Fast reps in the RDL miss the slow-stretch hypertrophy stimulus, which is the reason you do the lift.
fixTips: 3 seconds down, 1 second pause, 1 second up.
Pick a weight that forces tempo compliance, not a weight that survives it.
Use a metronome for 2 sessions to internalize the tempo.

entries.shortExplanation: Bar doesn't touch the chest.
entries.fullExplanation: Depth in the bench is a touch at the chest. Stopping short turns full-range bench into a half-range press.
entries.whyItMatters: Partial-range benching under-trains the pecs at length and teaches a rep pattern that skips their hardest point.
fixTips: Pause 1 second at the chest on every rep.
Use a foam-roller or Swiss-ball as a depth check until it's automatic.
Lower the weight 10% for two sessions and rebuild depth.

entries.shortExplanation: One side locks out before the other.
entries.fullExplanation: Asymmetric benches almost always come from a dominant side — the stronger side finishes first and then the weak side catches up.
entries.whyItMatters: A side-dominant bench compounds over years into an imbalance that limits your top weights.
fixTips: Swap 1 session/month to dumbbell bench to force per-side balance.
Film from head-on to see the asymmetry.
Add single-arm dumbbell floor press for the weak side.

entries.shortExplanation: Elbows flare wide (close to 90°) during the press.
entries.fullExplanation: Wide elbows put the shoulder in a vulnerable impingement position. Tucked elbows (~45° from torso) are shoulder-friendly.
entries.whyItMatters: Repeated flared benching is a leading cause of rotator-cuff irritation and AC joint problems.
fixTips: Cue: 'bend the bar apart' to activate lats and tuck elbows.
Narrower grip — just outside shoulder-width.
Add face pulls and band pull-aparts between pressing days.

entries.shortExplanation: Bar cadence is faster than the target tempo.
entries.fullExplanation: Fast benching usually means bouncing off the chest or racing the descent.
entries.whyItMatters: Bouncing and fast eccentrics hide weakness and train momentum, not strength.
fixTips: 3-second descent, 1-second pause, explosive press.
Drop the weight 10% to enforce tempo.
Pause-bench day every 2 weeks.

entries.shortExplanation: Arms don't fully extend during the hang.
entries.fullExplanation: A true dead hang has arms fully extended. Bending them shortens the hang and engages biceps to help support the load.
entries.whyItMatters: Bent arms shortcut the grip and shoulder stability benefits — the whole point of the hang.
fixTips: Lower hang duration and prioritize fully extended arms.
Use a lower bar where you can hold your full body weight without assistance.
Scapular pullups between hangs to reinforce proper positioning.

entries.shortExplanation: Shoulders pull up toward the ears.
entries.fullExplanation: A passive hang lets shoulders rise toward the ears. An active hang depresses the scapula — shoulders pulled down and back.
entries.whyItMatters: Passive hangs stress the shoulder capsule; active hangs train the shoulder girdle and build real carryover.
fixTips: Think: 'pull shoulders into back pockets' at the bottom.
Do active scap pullups to groove the pattern before hangs.
Reduce hang time if you can't maintain depression.

entries.shortExplanation: Hang duration is shorter than your target.
entries.fullExplanation: Hangs build grip endurance through time under tension. Short holds miss the adaptation.
entries.whyItMatters: Short hangs feel easy but don't build the grip capacity that longer hangs do.
fixTips: Set a target time before starting and honor it.
Practice one hang per day to the max, tracking time.
Don't chain together short hangs — one long hold is better.

entries.shortExplanation: Torso leans to one side under the load.
entries.fullExplanation: The stronger or heavier-loaded side pulls your torso sideways. A proper farmers walk keeps the torso upright, compensating with core bracing.
entries.whyItMatters: A leaning walk trains the obliques asymmetrically and reduces the core-bracing benefit.
fixTips: Brace the side where weight is pulling you toward.
Drop 10% and focus on upright posture.
Add side planks to warmup to wake the obliques.

entries.shortExplanation: Shoulders rise toward ears under the load.
entries.fullExplanation: Shrugged shoulders under heavy loads fatigue the upper traps fast and create neck tension.
entries.whyItMatters: Chronic shrug-carrying irritates the upper traps and neck — not what the farmers walk is training.
fixTips: Depress shoulders actively — think 'long neck, shoulders down'.
Lower load until you can maintain shoulder position for the full distance.
Add face pulls to strengthen scap retractors.

entries.shortExplanation: Torso leans forward under the load.
entries.fullExplanation: A forward lean puts weight onto the quads and lower back. A proper carry is upright — head stacked over hips.
entries.whyItMatters: Forward lean loads the lumbar spine unnecessarily and reduces the core-training value of the carry.
fixTips: Brace core before picking up the weights.
Cue: 'stack head over hips'.
Lower load if upright posture slips in the last 10 meters.

entries.shortExplanation: One shoulder sits higher than the other.
entries.fullExplanation: Asymmetric shoulder height under load often reveals a side dominance — the stronger side naturally lifts higher.
entries.whyItMatters: Long-term asymmetry can create shoulder impingement on the high side.
fixTips: Film from directly behind to see it.
Use a mirror or camera feedback during the first few steps.
Lower load to the weaker side's capacity.

entries.shortExplanation: Distance is shorter than your target.
entries.fullExplanation: Farmers walks build capacity through distance or time. Short carries miss the stimulus.
entries.whyItMatters: Cutting the carry short skips the fatigue-resistance adaptation.
fixTips: Set a distance goal before picking up the weights — and commit.
Drop weight to finish the target distance.
Track distance in a log to see progression.

entries.shortExplanation: You jerk the weights off the floor instead of deadlifting them.
entries.fullExplanation: The pickup matters — it's a mini-deadlift. Yanking the weights with a rounded back or split stance is a real injury path.
entries.whyItMatters: Most farmers-walk injuries happen on the pickup and setdown, not during the carry itself.
fixTips: Treat the pickup like a deadlift — brace, neutral spine, push the floor away.
Pause 1 second standing tall before starting to walk.
Set the weights down with the same care.
